skill.name: email
skill.description: Send, fetch, and read email through IMAP and SMTP using local shell scripts and curl. Credentials are read from macOS Keychain and account settings are stored in references/accounts.yaml.
skill.summary: Send, fetch, and read email through IMAP and SMTP with macOS Keychain credentials.
skill.author: 7Sageer, Claude

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly found shell scripts, network email operations, filesystem use, and macOS Keychain access. These behaviors match the stated purpose of an email client skill, and I found no prompt injection, hidden exfiltration endpoint, or confirmed malicious intent. The skill remains medium risk because it can read private email, use stored credentials, and send outbound messages.

medium_findings.title: Credential-Backed IMAP and SMTP Access
medium_findings.description: The scripts read email account passwords from macOS Keychain and pass them to curl for IMAP and SMTP connections. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it grants access to private mailbox data and outbound email capability.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The security command directly reads Keychain passwords, and curl receives those credentials for network mail protocols. The context confirms legitimate email access, not hidden theft.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Email Sending Without Script-Level Approval Gate
medium_findings.description: The send script composes an email from command-line arguments and sends it through SMTP. The documentation tells agents to confirm before sending, but the script itself does not enforce a confirmation step.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SMTP send path is explicit and operational. Risk depends on agent behavior because the confirmation requirement is guidance, not code enforcement.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated IMAP Request Arguments
medium_findings.description: Mailbox names and message IDs are accepted from command-line arguments and embedded into IMAP URLs or request strings. Quoting reduces shell injection risk, but malformed or crafted values can still affect remote IMAP commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are quoted for the local shell, so classic command injection is unlikely. They are still inserted into protocol-level requests without strict numeric or mailbox validation.

low_findings.title: Temporary Files Contain Email Content Briefly
low_findings.description: The scripts use temporary files to store outgoing message data and fetched email content before cleanup. This is a normal implementation pattern, but sensitive message text may exist on disk during execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The mktemp and cleanup behavior is clear in the scripts. The risk is limited because cleanup traps are present and no persistent storage path is used.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Personal Account Defaults
low_findings.description: The account configuration includes a real-looking default email address and provider settings. This is not credential exposure, but marketplace users must replace it before use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The account address and host settings are visible in configuration and documentation. No password is present, so the issue is privacy and configuration hygiene rather than credential leakage.

dangerous_patterns.title: macOS Keychain Password Retrieval
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill invokes security find-generic-password to retrieve IMAP and SMTP secrets for mail access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact Keychain command is present in executable code and user documentation. This is intentional but sensitive behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentialed Network Mail Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill performs IMAP and SMTP network operations with user credentials through curl.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network mail commands are explicit and use configured credentials. The endpoints are configured mail servers, not hidden third-party collectors.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic IMAP Request Construction
dangerous_patterns.description: The read script builds an IMAP FETCH request from a command-line email ID without strict validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The argument is not evaluated by the shell, but it is inserted into an IMAP request. Protocol-level validation is missing.

content.user_title: Send and Read Email from AI Workflows
content.value_statement: Email tasks often require switching tools and handling credentials by hand. This skill lets Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use local IMAP and SMTP scripts with Keychain-stored passwords.
actual_capabilities: Sends text email through SMTP over TLS using configured account settings.
Fetches recent IMAP message headers with sender, subject, date, and read status.
Reads specific messages as summaries, headers, body text, or full output.
Retrieves IMAP and SMTP passwords from macOS Keychain service entries.
Uses YAML account settings for provider host, port, login, and protocol values.
Decodes MIME headers and extracts readable plain text from email bodies.
limitations: Requires macOS Keychain and local shell access for credential retrieval.
Does not implement OAuth2, attachment handling, or rich HTML message composition.
Server-side IMAP search is not implemented; filtering is done from fetched headers.
The send script does not enforce a confirmation prompt before SMTP delivery.

use_cases.title: Send short follow-up messages
use_cases.description: Draft and send simple text emails from an AI-assisted workflow after checking the recipient and subject.

use_cases.title: Inspect mailbox state from scripts
use_cases.description: Fetch recent IMAP headers and read selected messages while debugging notifications or support workflows.

use_cases.title: Monitor important inbox updates
use_cases.description: Review recent messages from a configured mailbox and summarize relevant updates for a task queue.

prompt_templates.title: Check Recent Mail
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my configured inbox and summarize the five most recent messages by sender, subject, and date.

prompt_templates.title: Read One Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Read message number 42 from my inbox and give me a short summary with any action items.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Reply
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the latest message from Alice and draft a concise reply. Ask for confirmation before sending it.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Notify
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch the latest 50 message headers, find messages about invoices, summarize them, and prepare one update email for approval.

output: Message 128 is from billing@example.com about the June invoice.
Message 127 is from Alice about the project schedule.
Summary: The sender attached invoice details and asks for payment confirmation by Friday.
Suggested next step: confirm the invoice amount before replying.

output_examples.input: Send a status update to Sam after I approve it.
output: Draft prepared for Sam with subject: Project status update.
The message has not been sent.
Please confirm the recipient, subject, and body before delivery.

best_practices: Use app-specific passwords and store them only in macOS Keychain.
Confirm recipients, subject, and message body before sending any email.
Replace the sample account configuration before using the skill in production.
anti_patterns: Do not store mailbox passwords in YAML files, prompts, or shell history.
Do not send messages from automated prompts without human approval.
Do not read or summarize private email unless the user clearly requested it.

faq.question: Which email providers can this skill use?
faq.answer: It can use providers that expose IMAP and SMTP settings, such as Tencent Enterprise Mail, Gmail, Outlook, and custom servers.

faq.question: Where are passwords stored?
faq.answer: Passwords are stored in macOS Keychain service entries and are read by the local security command.

faq.question: Can it send attachments?
faq.answer: No. The current scripts support text bodies and file-based body text, but not MIME attachments.

faq.question: Does it support OAuth2?
faq.answer: No. The current implementation uses password-based IMAP and SMTP authentication.

faq.question: Can it search all email on the server?
faq.answer: No. The documented search flow fetches recent headers and filters them locally with command-line tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It can be published with a warning because its sensitive behavior is expected, documented, and not hidden.
